preamble: The shift from an alveolar click [ǃ] to an alveolar pulmonic stop [d] (or [t]) is a process known as 'click loss'. Clicks are complex consonants with two closures (one front, one velar). When a language simplifies its phonology, the velaric suction mechanism is often abandoned, leaving only the front release—which corresponds to a standard alveolar stop.

examples.note: In modern/urban varieties of these SW Bantu languages, clicks borrowed from Khoisan neighbors are being replaced by pulmonic coronal stops.
Internal click loss: words with historical clicks are simplified to coronal stops in contact or koineization contexts.
